42	type tocTransformer struct {
43		r renderer.Renderer
44	}

46	func (t *tocTransformer) Transform(n *ast.Document, reader text.Reader, pc parser.Context) {
47		if b, ok := pc.Get(tocEnableKey).(bool); !ok || !b {
48			return
49		}
50	
51		var (
52			toc         tableofcontents.Builder
53			tocHeading  = &tableofcontents.Heading{}
54			level       int
55			row         = -1
56			inHeading   bool
57			headingText bytes.Buffer
58		)
59	
60		if ids := pc.IDs().(stringValuesProvider).StringValues(); len(ids) > 0 {
61			toc.SetIdentifiers(ids)
62		}
63	
64		ast.Walk(n, func(n ast.Node, entering bool) (ast.WalkStatus, error) {
65			s := ast.WalkStatus(ast.WalkContinue)
66			if n.Kind() == ast.KindHeading {
67				if inHeading && !entering {
68					tocHeading.Title = sanitizeTOCHeadingTitle(headingText.String())
69					headingText.Reset()
70					toc.AddAt(tocHeading, row, level-1)
71					tocHeading = &tableofcontents.Heading{}
72					inHeading = false
73					return s, nil
74				}
75	
76				inHeading = true
77			}
78	
79			if !(inHeading && entering) {
80				return s, nil
81			}
82	
83			switch n.Kind() {
84			case ast.KindHeading:
85				heading := n.(*ast.Heading)
86				level = heading.Level
87	
88				if level == 1 || row == -1 {
89					row++
90				}
91	
92				id, found := heading.AttributeString("id")
93				if found {
94					tocHeading.ID = string(id.([]byte))
95					tocHeading.Level = level
96				}
97			case
98				ast.KindCodeSpan,
99				ast.KindLink,
100				ast.KindImage,
101				ast.KindEmphasis,
102				strikethroughAst.KindStrikethrough,
103				emojiAst.KindEmoji,
104				extras.KindDelete,
105				extras.KindInsert,
106				extras.KindMark,
107				extras.KindSubscript,
108				extras.KindSuperscript:
109				err := t.r.Render(&headingText, reader.Source(), n)
110				if err != nil {
111					return s, err
112				}
113	
114				return ast.WalkSkipChildren, nil
115			case
116				ast.KindAutoLink,
117				ast.KindRawHTML,
118				ast.KindText,
119				ast.KindString:
120				err := t.r.Render(&headingText, reader.Source(), n)
121				if err != nil {
122					return s, err
123				}
124			}
125	
126			return s, nil
127		})
128	
129		pc.Set(tocResultKey, toc.Build())
130	}

137		return &tocExtension{

139		}
140	}

142	func (e *tocExtension) Extend(m goldmark.Markdown) {
143		r := goldmark.DefaultRenderer()
144		r.AddOptions(e.options...)
145		m.Parser().AddOptions(parser.WithASTTransformers(util.Prioritized(&tocTransformer{
146			r: r,
147		},
148			// This must run after the ID generation (priority 100).
149			110)))
150	}
151	
152	var tocSanitizerPolicy = newTOCSanitizerPolicy()
153	
154	// newTOCSanitizerPolicy returns a bluemonday policy for sanitizing TOC heading
155	// titles against an allowlist of inline HTML elements and attributes,
156	// specifically excluding anchor elements to prevent links within TOC heading
157	// titles.
158	func newTOCSanitizerPolicy() *bluemonday.Policy {
159		p := bluemonday.NewPolicy()
160		p.AllowElements(
161			"abbr", "b", "bdi", "bdo", "br", "cite", "code", "data", "del", "dfn",
162			"em", "i", "ins", "kbd", "mark", "q", "rp", "rt", "ruby", "s", "samp",
163			"small", "span", "strong", "sub", "sup", "time", "u", "var", "wbr",
164		)
165		p.AllowStandardAttributes()
166		p.AllowStyling()
167		p.AllowImages()
168		p.AllowAttrs("cite").OnElements("del", "ins", "q")
169		p.AllowAttrs("datetime").OnElements("del", "ins", "time")
170		p.AllowAttrs("value").OnElements("data")
171		return p
172	}
173	
174	var whiteSpaceRe = regexp.MustCompile(`\s+`)
175	
176	// sanitizeTOCHeadingTitle sanitizes s for use as a TOC heading title.
177	func sanitizeTOCHeadingTitle(s string) string {
178		if strings.IndexByte(s, '<') == -1 {
179			return s
180		}
181	
182		// Sanitize the string.
183		ss := tocSanitizerPolicy.Sanitize(s)
184	
185		// Remove extraneous whitespace.
186		return whiteSpaceRe.ReplaceAllString(strings.TrimSpace(ss), " ")
187	}

content.text:
Now I can see the issue. The TOC transformer creates a default Goldmark renderer (`goldmark.DefaultRenderer()`) which only understands the standard node kinds. When it encounters a node from an extension like Passthrough, the renderer panics because the node's Kind ID is an index into the renderer's function array that's out of bounds.

Let me look at how Goldmark's DefaultRenderer works and how extensions register node renderers.

content.content: markup/goldmark/toc.go:143:	r := goldmark.DefaultRenderer()

content.text: Let me check the goldmark internal code to understand the renderer structure better

Now I can see the issue clearly. On line 164 of `renderer.go`, Goldmark's `Render` method accesses `r.nodeRendererFuncs[n.Kind()]` without bounds checking. When a node kind from an extension (like Passthrough with Kind ID 41) is encountered by the default renderer (which only knows about standard node kinds up to a lower index), it causes an index out of range panic.

The fix should be in `toc.go` - the TOC renderer needs to handle this by either:
1. Adding bounds checking to `t.r.Render()` call
2. Or simply falling through/walking children of unknown node types

Let me check how the Passthrough extension works and look for existing tests.

Now I have a clear picture. The Passthrough extension registers its renderers on the main Goldmark renderer (`m.Renderer()`) but the TOC extension creates its own separate default renderer that doesn't have these extension renderers registered.

When the TOC renderer encounters a `PassthroughInline` node inside an Emphasis node (like `**$a$**` → Emphasis containing `$a$` PassthroughInline), it panics because `KindPassthroughInline` index is out of bounds for the default renderer's `nodeRendererFuncs` array.

The fix should be in `toc.go`. The TOC transformer's approach of calling `t.r.Render` on container nodes like `Emphasis` is fundamentally problematic because the renderer recursively walks into children that may have unknown node kinds.

The best approach: Instead of delegating to `t.r.Render` for container nodes (which then recurses into potentially unknown children), we should handle each container node inline in the TOC walker - writing the opening/closing HTML tags directly and letting the TOC walker handle the children (which will gracefully skip unknown node types).

content.text: Now let me think about the right fix. The core issue is in `toc.go` lines 97-123. For nodes like Emphasis, the code calls `t.r.Render()` which recursively walks children via the Goldmark renderer. If any child has a node kind not registered in the renderer, it panics. The fix: Don't use `t.r.Render` for container nodes. Instead, handle them inline. Looking at what `t.r.Render` does for these nodes in the HTML renderer: - Emphasis: ``/`` - Strikethrough: ``/`` (or ``) - Delete: ``/`` - Insert: ``/`` - Mark: ``/`` - Subscript: ``/`` - Superscript: ``/`` For CodeSpan and Emoji, they are leaf nodes — but the renderer also handles CodeSpan specially. Let me think about this more carefully. Actually, I think there's a much simpler and more robust approach. The problem is that `t.r.Render` walks children of the node being rendered. For container nodes (Emphasis, Link, Image, Strikethrough, extras), when the renderer encounters an unknown child node kind, it panics. Instead of using the renderer for container nodes, we can render them manually and let the TOC AST walker handle the children (including gracefully ignoring unknown node types). Here's my plan for the `toc.go` fix: 1. For container nodes (Emphasis, Link, Strikethrough, extras Delete/Insert/Mark/Sub/Sup), manually write the opening/closing HTML tags and do NOT skip children, so the TOC walker handles children. 2. For leaf nodes (CodeSpan, Emoji, Text, String, AutoLink, RawHTML), continue using `t.r.Render` since they have no children and won't cause the problem. 3. For Image, it's also a leaf, so it can stay. Let me implement this:

content.text:
Now I understand the pattern. The renderers for container inline nodes (Emphasis, Strikethrough, Link, extras tags) all have the same pattern: they write opening/HTML tags on entering, closing tags on exiting, and return `ast.WalkContinue` — meaning the renderer then recurses into children. The TOC walker handles children of heading nodes by itself (that's what lines 79-81 do). The issue is only when we call `t.r.Render` and it into child nodes with unknown kinds. Here's my plan: Instead of calling `t.r.Render` for container nodes and then using `ast.WalkSkipChildren`, I'll handle container nodes inline - manually write the opening HTML tags, let the TOC walker naturally handle the children, and then handle the closing tags. But wait — the current pattern is that `t.r.Render` is called on entering nodes only. The renderer handles both entering and exiting by doing `ast.Walk(n, ...)` which calls the render function with `entering=true` then `entering=false`. Since we're calling `t.r.Render` on entering only, the renderer handles the full subtree (entering + exiting + all children). For the fix, I need to handle both the entering and exiting phases of container nodes manually. Let me think about this differently. The simplest fix: For container nodes (Emphasis, Strikethrough, Link, extras), instead of calling `t.r.Render`, I'll: 1. Write the opening tag to `headingText` 2. NOT skip children (let the TOC walker handle them) 3. On exiting (when the walker comes back to this node), write the closing tag But the current code structure doesn't handle exiting for these nodes. The `if !(inHeading && entering)` check at line 79 filters them out on exiting. I think the simplest and most robust fix is: for ALL inline nodes, we handle them ourselves without calling `t.r.Render`. The renderer is the source of the problem, so we eliminate it from the equation. For `## $a$ inside heading`, the TOC shows `inside heading` — the `$a$` is being stripped by the passthrough. But since our TOC renderer doesn't have the passthrough renderer registered, the passthrough content is just skipped, which is fine for TOC purposes. 3. 171	// newTOCSanitizerPolicy returns a bluemonday policy for sanitizing TOC heading
172	// titles against an allowlist of inline HTML elements and attributes,
173	// specifically excluding anchor elements to prevent links within TOC heading
174	// titles.
175	func newTOCSanitizerPolicy() *bluemonday.Policy {
176		p := bluemonday.NewPolicy()
177		p.AllowElements(
178			"abbr", "b", "bdi", "bdo", "br", "cite", "code", "data", "del", "dfn",
179			"em", "i", "ins", "kbd", "mark", "q", "rp", "rt", "ruby", "s", "samp",
180			"small", "span", "strong", "sub", "sup", "time", "u", "var", "wbr",
181		)
182		p.AllowStandardAttributes()
183		p.AllowStyling()
184		p.AllowImages()
185		p.AllowAttrs("cite").OnElements("del", "ins", "q")
186		p.AllowAttrs("datetime").OnElements("del", "ins", "time")
187		p.AllowAttrs("value").OnElements("data")
188		return p
189	}

193	// writeOpeningInlineTag writes the opening HTML tag for an inline container node.
194	func writeOpeningInlineTag(w *bytes.Buffer, n ast.Node) {
195		tag := inlineTagName(n.Kind(), n)
196		if tag == "" {
197			return
198		}
199		fmt.Fprintf(w, "<%s>", tag)
200	}
201	
202	// writeClosingInlineTag writes the closing HTML tag for an inline container node.
203	func writeClosingInlineTag(w *bytes.Buffer, n ast.Node) {
204		tag := inlineTagName(n.Kind(), n)
205		if tag == "" {
206			return
207		}
208		fmt.Fprintf(w, "</%s>", tag)
209	}

211	// inlineTagName returns the HTML tag name for a given inline node kind.
212	func inlineTagName(kind ast.NodeKind, n ast.Node) string {
213		switch kind {
214		case ast.KindEmphasis:
215			if n.(*ast.Emphasis).Level == 2 {
216				return "strong"
217			}
218			return "em"
219		case strikethroughAst.KindStrikethrough:
220			return "del"
221		case extras.KindDelete:
222			return "del"
223		case extras.KindInsert:
224			return "ins"
225		case extras.KindMark:
226			return "mark"
227		case extras.KindSubscript:
228			return "sub"
229		case extras.KindSuperscript:
230			return "sup"
231		default:
232			return ""
233		}
234	}

236	// sanitizeTOCHeadingTitle sanitizes s for use as a TOC heading title.
237	func sanitizeTOCHeadingTitle(s string) string {
238		if strings.IndexByte(s, '<') == -1 {
239			return s
240		}
241	
242		// Sanitize the string.
243		ss := tocSanitizerPolicy.Sanitize(s)
244	
245		// Remove extraneous whitespace.
246		return whiteSpaceRe.ReplaceAllString(strings.TrimSpace(ss), " ")
247	}

content.text:
The bug has been fixed. Here's a summary of the changes:

## Root Cause

The TOC transformer in `markup/goldmark/toc.go` creates a `goldmark.DefaultRenderer()` which only has standard Goldmark node renderers registered. When it encounters inline container nodes (like `Emphasis`) containing children from extension node types (like `PassthroughInline`), the renderer's internal walk panics with an index out of range because the extension node kind ID exceeds the renderer's function array size.

## Fix

Modified `toc.go` to separate the handling of **container** inline nodes from **leaf** inline nodes:

1. **Container inline nodes** (`Emphasis`, `Strikethrough`, `Delete`, `Insert`, `Mark`, `Subscript`, `Superscript`, `Link`) are now handled directly by the TOC walker rather than delegated to `t.r.Render`. The walker writes the opening/closing HTML tags inline (for nodes where tags are relevant) and lets its own AST walk handle children — which gracefully ignores unknown node types by falling through the switch without rendering.

2. **Leaf inline nodes** (`CodeSpan`, `Image`, `Emoji`, `AutoLink`, `RawHTML`, `Text`, `String`) continue to use `t.r.Render` since they have no children and are safe from this class of panic.

3. Added helper functions `inlineTagName`, `writeOpeningInlineTag`, and `writeClosingInlineTag` to handle tag output for the container nodes.
